elements.text: Nagagami Forest
Project highlight
On this project we will be heading 130 km into the bush, deep into the Nagagami Forest.

After planting 1.65 million trees in this neck of the woods last year, Integrity Reforestation will be returning to the Nagagami Forest to plant an additional 1.5 million trees in areas harvested between 2023 and 2025.
50 of the hardest working tree planters in Ontario will spend 20 days here.
We setup camp in close proximity to the worksites and work full days, planting each tree by hand, targeting the best microsites, to achieve the best survival results.

Trees include Jack Pine, Black Spruce and White Spruce targeting previously 
confir-dominant stands.
Tree planting these sites attempts to bring them back to the species that existed there before the stand was cut.
Every tree gets assessed and must meet minimum 90% quality specifications met or get replanted
Quality checkers examine the trees by walking the planted land and throwing 3.99 meter radius plots, recording quality data and determining pass or fail tree quality.

bfae2528-6d98-41f5-9821-d3d43b7c53e3.text: "I started the company because I love tree planting and the formative impact it has on the environment and the people that do this work, including me."

f2f2d743-4082-4fd5-a921-d88903a16960.text: "I think the experience working as a tree planter sets up a person for success in life."

1c2d0e38-c5c6-4c51-9556-4941a48bc509.text: "I see Integrity's role as creating the conditions necessary for tree planters to thrive."

9591c027-c617-4905-8bda-df0dd2297fa7.text: "I was also that kid that was always dirty, had ripped jeans, covered in mud, so tree planting is right up my alley."

f35c6417-340b-40cc-bce4-5ac9f983aef7.text: "I have been doing this work for 18 years and still love it."

809d53a5-6295-4f62-b1e5-490605c4c960.text:
Matt McKernan, 
Founder of Integrity Reforestation

ba93ab96-ef40-4c6e-a65d-5ce012954f22.text: Last July, we planted 2.2 million trees in the Ogoki, to help restore some of the 49,000 hectares of land that burned in the 2023 wildfires.

1592e802-35d5-40aa-8299-8d285856d97b.text: To continue the work restoring this land, we will plan an addition 1.3 million trees here in July 2026.

elements.text: Ogoki Forest
Project highlight
This season we fly 160 km north into the Ogoki watershed — one of Ontario's most remote boreal systems.
Integrity Reforestation returns to the Ogoki Forest to plant 1.2 million trees across cutblocks harvested between 2022 and 2024, accessible only by helicopter.
45 planters will spend 18 days working these remote cutblocks, crossing rivers and working some of the most challenging terrain in the province.
Camp is established riverside and the crew works full days from first light, targeting the best microsites to maximise seedling survival through Ontario winters.
Black Spruce, Jack Pine and Balsam Fir planted to restore previously conifer-dominant stands.
Species selection follows OMNRF block prescriptions, matching the native composition of each harvested stand to give every tree the best long-term survival rate.
Every tree is assessed against OMNRF minimum 85% quality standards — or it gets replanted.
Our quality team walks the planted land daily, running 3.99m radius plot checks and recording pass/fail data to ensure every block meets contract specifications before we leave.
Kenora 20 Wildfire
Project highlight
In May 2025 a wildfire in Ingolf, Ontario burned 36,043 hectares — the largest wildfire in Ontario that year.
The fire is reported in the Kenora Forest, an area of land managed in partnership with eight First Nations communities.
850 members of Wabaseemoong First Nation were evacuated to Niagara Falls as the fire reached 23,281 hectares in just two days.
Over 150 firefighters, helicopters and CL-415 water bombers fought the blaze from the air.
The fire peaked at 31,276 hectares before firefighting efforts slowed its growth and residents were permitted to return home.
Crews continued to monitor for hot spots and suppression work persisted for weeks after the evacuation order was lifted.
Our crews performed the first site assessment — walking the land and flying drones to map burn severity across the entire affected area.
We found wide variability in burn severity. Some areas will be hand-planted. Others show enough natural seed source to regenerate on their own — and we will leave them to do exactly that.
Kenora 20 — The Plan
471,600 trees. One mission.
471,600 trees planted by hand across two sites in summer 2026.
Integrity is contracted to restore Kenora 20 starting summer 2026 — bringing experienced crews and proven planting methods to one of Ontario's largest post-fire sites.
The Right Tree in the Right Place

Red Pine — thrives in well-drained sandy soils

Jack Pine — built for sandy, full-sun conditions

White Spruce — suited to richer soils and shade

Species selection is guided by drone surveys and on-the-ground site assessment.
Every hectare is assessed before a single seedling goes in.
Burn severity varies widely across Kenora 20. High-severity zones receive intensive hand-planting. Areas with natural regeneration potential are left to recover on their own.
Restoring what fire took.
Follow along as we plant 471,600 trees in the Kenora Forest — one seedling at a time.
Kenora 20 — The Partnership
First Nations-led reforestation.
Kenora 20 is managed by the people who know this land best.

Miitigoog LP is a First Nations-owned organization uniting 8 partner nations across the Kenora region. They lead sustainable forest management and resource stewardship on their traditional territories.
8 Nations. One Forest.

Wabaseemoong Independent Nations
Naotkamegwanning First Nation
Niisaachewan Anishinaabe Nation
Ojibways of Onigaming
Northwest Angle #33
Shoal Lake #40
Anishnabeg of Naongashing
Wabauskang First Nation
We plant alongside communities who have tended this land for generations.
Integrity Reforestation embeds within the Miitigoog operation — bringing specialized planting crews, seedling logistics, and ground-crew experience to support the Nations' restoration vision.
Land back. Forest forward.
Follow our work restoring the Kenora 20 fire site in partnership with Miitigoog LP.
